nodes.name: Cooking Terms & Techniques
nodes.description: French and international culinary terminology and techniques

items.prompt: What French culinary phrase, literally meaning 'everything in its place,' describes the practice of preparing and organizing all ingredients and equipment before cooking begins?
items.explanation:
French: 'mise' (putting/placing) + 'en place' (in place) — the foundational discipline of professional kitchens
Refers to measuring, chopping, portioning, and organizing all ingredients before the heat goes on
Allows cooks to work cleanly and quickly during service without stopping to prep mid-cook
Institutionalized in classical French brigade kitchens under Escoffier in the late 19th century
Considered a mental discipline as much as a physical one — the organized cook is the calm cook
Professional chefs often use the term to describe a broader philosophy of preparedness and organization in life
New cooks skipping mise en place is the most common cause of burning, mistiming, or forgetting ingredients during service
items.cardBack: Everything in its place (prep before cooking)

items.prompt: What knife cut produces thin, uniform matchstick strips of vegetables approximately 1/8 inch × 1/8 inch × 2–3 inches, often used for stir-fries, garnishes, and salads?
items.explanation:
Classic French knife cut: 1/8 inch × 1/8 inch × 2–3 inch strips, resembling wooden matchsticks
Achieved by cutting the vegetable into planks, then stacking planks and slicing into strips
Brunoise is made from julienned strips cut crosswise into 1/8-inch cubes
Fine julienne (chiffonade of firm vegetables) can be 1/16 inch thick
Common vegetables for julienne: carrots, zucchini, leek, bell peppers, daikon, cucumber
The word may derive from the French name 'Julien' or 'Jules' — origin uncertain; used in French culinary texts since at least the 18th century
A mandoline speeds up julienning; many have a julienne blade attachment

items.prompt: What French cooking technique uses a container of water to provide gentle, indirect heat for delicate preparations like custards, sauces, and melted chocolate, and takes its name from a 1st–3rd century CE Alexandrian alchemist?
items.explanation:
French: 'Mary's bath' — from medieval Latin balneum Mariae, named for Mary the Jewess, an early alchemist credited with inventing the double boiler-like apparatus
Mary the Jewess (1st–3rd century CE, Alexandria) is considered the first Western alchemist; Zosimos of Panopolis (300 CE) credits her with the invention
In cooking: a bowl or pan set over simmering water (double boiler) or a baking dish placed in a roasting pan of hot water in the oven
Maximum temperature achieved is ~100°C (212°F), the boiling point of water — prevents overheating and curdling
Used for: chocolate tempering, hollandaise, crème brûlée, cheesecakes, terrines, custards
In food service: a bain-marie also refers to a hot water bath used to keep prepared foods warm on a buffet line
The name jumped from alchemy to culinary literature as chemistry and cooking shared vocabulary in the early modern period

items.prompt: What mixture of equal parts fat and flour, cooked together before adding liquid, forms the thickening base for French mother sauces like béchamel, velouté, and espagnole?
items.explanation:
Fat (butter, oil, or lard) cooked with flour to form a paste that thickens liquids when a liquid is whisked in
Three stages by color: white roux (1–2 minutes, béchamel base), blonde roux (5–6 minutes, velouté base), brown/dark roux (10–30+ minutes, espagnole base, or Cajun/Creole gumbo)
The longer the roux cooks, the more flavor it develops — but the less thickening power it retains (starches break down)
Cajun and Creole cooking use dark, oil-based roux cooked 30–45 minutes until 'chocolate colored' for gumbo
Lumps form when cold roux meets cold liquid or hot roux meets hot liquid too fast; one should be warm, the other cool
The ratio of fat:flour:liquid determines sauce thickness; classic ratio is 1:1 fat to flour by weight
Escoffier's five French mother sauces (béchamel, velouté, espagnole, hollandaise, and sauce tomat) include three roux-based sauces
items.cardBack: Fat + flour cooked together (base for mother sauces)

items.prompt: What aromatic vegetable mixture of onion, carrot, and celery — combined in a 2:1:1 ratio by weight — forms the flavor foundation of stocks, braises, and soups across classical French cuisine?
items.explanation:
Classic French aromatic base: 2 parts onion, 1 part carrot, 1 part celery by weight
Named after Charles de Lévis, Duke of Mirepoix (18th century); the technique was already established — the name is an honorific
Diced size varies by intended cooking time: large dice for long braises/stocks, small dice (brunoise) for quick sauces
Cooking in fat releases fat-soluble flavor compounds; sweating (no browning) or caramelizing changes the flavor profile
White mirepoix (mirepox blanc): replaces carrots with parsnip and leek to avoid color in light stocks and sauces
The Holy Trinity of Cajun/Creole cooking (onion, bell pepper, celery) is the American equivalent
Soffritto (Italian): onion, carrot, celery sautéed in olive oil; Sofrito (Spanish/Latin): tomato, peppers, onion, garlic

items.prompt: What precise knife cut produces fine 1/8-inch cubes, achieved by first making julienne strips and then cutting them crosswise, used for garnishes, soups, and sauces that require uniform cooking?
items.explanation:
Fine dice: 1/8 inch × 1/8 inch × 1/8 inch cubes; derived from julienned strips cut crosswise
Fine brunoise: 1/16 inch × 1/16 inch × 1/16 inch — requires exceptional knife skill
Used where visual precision and uniform cooking are critical: consommé garnish, sauce finishes, canapé toppings
Name is French; etymology uncertain — possibly from 'brunir' (to brown) or a regional term
The smaller the cut, the faster the vegetable cooks and the more surface area is exposed
Standard dice sizes: brunoise (1/8 in.), small dice (1/4 in.), medium dice (1/2 in.), large dice (3/4 in.)
Professional culinary knife skills tests often include a timed brunoise to assess precision and speed

items.prompt: What French cooking method, whose name literally means 'to jump,' involves cooking food quickly in a small amount of fat over high heat while keeping it in motion?
items.explanation:
French: 'sauter' = to jump; refers to tossing or stirring food in a hot pan to prevent burning and ensure even browning
High heat + small amount of fat + constant motion = rapid cooking that develops a Maillard reaction crust
Foods must be dry before sautéing; moisture causes steaming instead of browning
A sauté pan (straight-sided) differs from a sauteuse (sloped sides for flipping) — both are used for this technique
Distinct from stir-fry (Chinese wok, very high heat, constant stirring) and pan-fry (more oil, less movement)
Maillard reaction: browning of amino acids and sugars at ~140°C+, creating hundreds of new flavor compounds
Sautéed food should be in a single layer with space between pieces — crowding causes steaming and prevents browning
items.cardBack: Quick cooking in fat over high heat ('to jump')

items.prompt: What two-stage cooking technique briefly plunges food into boiling water and then immediately transfers it to an ice bath, setting color, partially cooking vegetables, or removing bitterness?
items.explanation:
Stage 1: brief boiling water submersion (30 seconds to several minutes depending on the food)
Stage 2: immediate transfer to an ice water bath (shocking) to halt carryover cooking and preserve color
Sets chlorophyll in green vegetables, keeping them vibrant; without shocking, green vegetables turn drab olive
Also used to: remove skin from tomatoes and stone fruits, reduce bitterness in bitter greens, loosen almond skins
Required before freezing most vegetables to inactivate enzymes that cause deterioration in the freezer
Difference from parboiling: parboiling is partial cooking without the ice bath; blanching always includes the shock step
The term comes from French 'blanchir' (to whiten) — originally referred to blanching white meats in boiling water
items.cardBack: Brief boiling then ice bath (sets color, stops cooking)

items.prompt: What knife technique produces thin ribbon-like strips of leafy herbs or greens (such as basil or spinach) by stacking and rolling the leaves tightly before slicing crosswise?
items.explanation:
French: 'chiffon' (rag/ribbon) + '-ade' (made of) — resulting in very thin, ribbon-like strips
Process: stack leaves, roll tightly into a cigar shape, slice crosswise into thin strips
Used primarily on delicate leaves: basil, mint, sorrel, spinach, sage, radicchio, cabbage for coleslaw
Basil chiffonade should never be refrigerated after cutting — the cold blackens (oxidizes) the cut edges
Width varies by use: very thin for garnish, slightly wider for salad incorporation
For firmer leaves (kale, cabbage), remove the stem first; for delicate leaves (basil), keep stems intact during rolling then discard
A chiffonade of basil is the classic garnish for Caprese salad, margherita pizza, and tomato sauces
items.cardBack: Thin ribbon strips of leafy herbs or greens

items.prompt: What technique adds liquid (wine, stock, or water) to a hot pan after searing, loosening the caramelized browned bits (called fond) stuck to the bottom to build a flavorful pan sauce?
items.explanation:
The browned bits are called fond (from French for 'bottom' or 'base', rooted in Latin fundus) — concentrated Maillard reaction flavors from searing
The liquid sizzles and steams on contact, releasing the fond from the pan through moisture and physical stirring
Common deglazing liquids: red or white wine, stock (chicken, beef, veal), brandy, beer, fruit juice, or water
Alcohol deglazed over high heat can briefly catch flame (flambé) if splashed on an open burner flame
After deglazing, the liquid is reduced to concentrate flavors; butter or cream can be swirled in to finish the sauce
Pan sauces built this way are called 'jus lié' or simply 'pan drippings sauce'
Deglazing is central to French technique: Beef Bourguignon, Coq au Vin, and most braises start with a deglaze step
items.cardBack: Liquid added to hot pan to loosen fond (browned bits)

items.prompt: What process binds two normally immiscible liquids (fat and water) into a stable mixture using an emulsifier, explaining how sauces like hollandaise, mayonnaise, and vinaigrette hold together?
items.explanation:
Immiscible: liquids that naturally separate (oil and water); emulsification forces them into stable suspension
Emulsifiers have hydrophilic (water-loving) and hydrophobic (fat-loving) ends that coat fat droplets and keep them dispersed
Egg yolk is the key emulsifier in hollandaise and mayonnaise — lecithin in yolk stabilizes fat-in-water emulsions
Mayonnaise: oil whisked into egg yolk + acid (lemon/vinegar) to create a stable oil-in-water emulsion
Hollandaise: egg yolks whisked with clarified butter over a bain-marie; broken hollandaise can be rescued by whisking a broken sauce into a fresh yolk
A temporary emulsion (vinaigrette) separates on standing; a permanent emulsion (mayonnaise) stays stable indefinitely
Mustard acts as an additional emulsifier in many vinaigrettes and sauces
items.cardBack: Binding oil and water with an emulsifier (hollandaise, mayo)

items.prompt: What French cooking technique seals food (often fish or vegetables) in a folded parchment paper parcel that puffs and steams during cooking, presented at the table for a dramatic tableside opening?
items.explanation:
French: 'en papillote' means 'in parchment' or 'in a paper case'; 'papillon' means butterfly
Food is placed on half of a parchment sheet with aromatics, herbs, butter, and a splash of liquid; the other half is folded over and crimped to seal
The sealed parcel traps steam, creating a gentle self-basting environment inside — preserving moisture and infusing flavor
The parcel puffs dramatically in the oven; presenting it at the table and cutting it open releases a burst of fragrant steam
Fish and shellfish are ideal (delicate, quick-cooking); chicken breast and vegetables also work well
Al cartoccio is the Italian equivalent; occasionally foil is used instead of parchment for durability
The technique dates at least to medieval Arab cuisine; popularized in French fine dining by the 19th century
items.cardBack: Cooking in sealed parchment parcel (steaming in paper)

items.prompt: What Japanese-named 'fifth taste,' identified by chemist Kikunae Ikeda in 1908 as the savory flavor of glutamate, joins sweet, salty, sour, and bitter as a basic human taste sensation?
items.explanation:
Coined by Kikunae Ikeda of Tokyo Imperial University in 1908 after isolating glutamic acid from kombu (kelp) dashi
Japanese: 'umai' (delicious) + 'mi' (taste) — often translated as 'savory' or 'pleasant savoriness'
Primary compound: monosodium glutamate (MSG), which Ikeda commercialized as Aji-no-moto (essence of taste) after patenting the production method
Glutamate-rich foods: aged Parmesan, soy sauce, miso, tomatoes (especially cooked), anchovies, mushrooms, fish sauce, kombu
Took nearly a century to gain broad scientific acceptance; umami receptors on the tongue were confirmed in 2002
Fifth taste joins sweet, salty, sour, and bitter — some researchers propose a sixth taste for fat (oleogustus)
Umami is not just MSG: free glutamates in slow-cooked foods develop naturally through protein breakdown
items.cardBack: Fifth taste / savory (glutamate, named 1908 by Ikeda)

items.prompt: What Italian phrase, literally meaning 'to the tooth,' describes pasta or rice cooked just until it offers slight resistance when bitten, rather than being fully soft all the way through?
items.explanation:
Italian: 'al dente' = 'to the tooth'; describes the texture of pasta that is cooked but retains a slight firmness at its center
The center should show a tiny white dot when a strand is cut — just barely undercooked at the core
Significance: pasta continues cooking from residual heat and when finished in sauce; finishing in the pan al dente produces perfectly cooked final texture
Overcooked pasta loses structure because starch granules absorb too much water and swell excessively
The term applies beyond pasta: rice (especially in risotto), vegetables, and legumes are also described as al dente
Risotto should have al dente rice grains in a creamy, starchy sauce — overcooking produces a mushy porridge
Packaged pasta cooking times are generally 1–2 minutes longer than optimal for al dente; test by tasting, not the clock

items.prompt: What technique, used in chocolate-making and egg cookery, involves controlled heating and cooling to achieve a specific molecular structure — either stabilizing cocoa butter crystals in chocolate or preventing egg curdling in hot mixtures?
items.explanation:
In chocolate: controlled heating and cooling sequence to crystallize cocoa butter in its Form V (beta-prime) crystal structure — gives properly tempered chocolate its snap, gloss, and smooth melt
Tempered chocolate process: melt to ~45°C, cool to ~27°C (seeding with crystals), rewarm to ~31°C for use; technique varies by type (dark, milk, white)
Untempered chocolate blooms (gray-white streaks) as cocoa butter re-crystallizes in an unstable form
In egg cookery: tempering means gradually whisking hot liquid into egg yolks before adding yolks to the pan — prevents scrambling (curdling) by slowly raising the egg temperature
Applied to custards (crème anglaise, pastry cream), carbonara, and hollandaise
The word 'temper' comes from Latin temperare (to moderate, restrain) — controlling conditions to achieve a specific result
Improperly tempered chocolate (e.g., seized chocolate from water contact) becomes a grainy, unusable paste
items.cardBack: Controlled heating/cooling (chocolate crystals or egg cookery)
